35:   sourceDiversity: number;
36:   evidenceDepth: number;
37: }
38: 
39: export interface ReviewConvergenceSignals {
40:   dimensionCoverage: number;
41:   findingStability: number;
42:   p0ResolutionRate: number;
43:   evidenceDensity: number;
44:   hotspotSaturation: number;
45: }
46: 
47: export interface ContextConvergenceSignals {
48:   sliceCoverage: number;
49:   reuseCatalogCoverage: number;
50:   agreementRate: number;
51:   relevanceFloor: number;
52:   dependencyCompleteness: number;
53: }
54: 
55: export type ConvergenceSignals =
56:   | ResearchConvergenceSignals
57:   | ReviewConvergenceSignals
58:   | ContextConvergenceSignals;
59: 
60: export interface SignalSnapshot {
61:   iteration: number;
62:   signals: ConvergenceSignals;
63:   nodeSignals: NodeSignal[];
64:   nodeCount: number;
65:   edgeCount: number;
66: }
67: 
68: // ───── CONSTANTS ─────
69: 
70: type ResearchSignalNodeLike = {
71:   id: string;
72:   kind: string;
73:   metadata?: CoverageNode['metadata'] | string | null;
74: };
75: 
76: type ResearchSignalEdgeLike = {
77:   sourceId: string;
78:   targetId: string;
79:   relation: string;
80: };
81: 
82: interface SqlFragment {
83:   clause: string;
84:   params: unknown[];
85: }
86: 
87: // ───── HELPERS ─────
88: 
89: function buildNamespacePredicate(alias: string, ns: Namespace): SqlFragment {
90:   const prefix = alias ? `${alias}.` : '';
91:   const clauses = [`${prefix}spec_folder = ?`, `${prefix}loop_type = ?`];
92:   const params: unknown[] = [ns.specFolder, ns.loopType];
93: 
94:   if (ns.sessionId) {
95:     clauses.push(`${prefix}session_id = ?`);
96:     params.push(ns.sessionId);
97:   }
98: 
99:   return {
100:     clause: clauses.join(' AND '),
101:     params,
102:   };
103: }
104: 
105: function buildCompositeNodeJoin(
106:   nodeAlias: string,
107:   edgeAlias: string,
108:   edgeNodeColumn: 'source_id' | 'target_id',
109: ): string {
110:   return `${nodeAlias}.spec_folder = ${edgeAlias}.spec_folder
111:       AND ${nodeAlias}.loop_type = ${edgeAlias}.loop_type
112:       AND ${nodeAlias}.session_id = ${edgeAlias}.session_id
113:       AND ${nodeAlias}.id = ${edgeAlias}.${edgeNodeColumn}`;
114: }
115: 
116: function parseNodeMetadata(metadata: CoverageNode['metadata'] | string | null | undefined): Record<string, unknown> | null {
117:   if (!metadata) return null;
118:   if (typeof metadata === 'string') {
119:     try {
120:       const parsed = JSON.parse(metadata) as unknown;
121:       return parsed && typeof parsed === 'object' ? parsed as Record<string, unknown> : null;
122:     } catch {
123:       return null;
124:     }
125:   }
126:   return typeof metadata === 'object' ? metadata as Record<string, unknown> : null;
127: }
128: 
129: function buildAnsweringFindingsByQuestion(edges: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalEdgeLike>): Map<string, string[]> {
130:   const answeringFindings = new Map<string, string[]>();
131: 
132:   for (const edge of edges) {
133:     if (edge.relation !== 'ANSWERS') continue;
134:     if (!answeringFindings.has(edge.targetId)) answeringFindings.set(edge.targetId, []);
135:     answeringFindings.get(edge.targetId)!.push(edge.sourceId);
136:   }
137: 
138:   return answeringFindings;
139: }
140: 
141: function buildCitedSourcesByFinding(edges: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalEdgeLike>): Map<string, string[]> {
142:   const citedSources = new Map<string, string[]>();
143: 
144:   for (const edge of edges) {
145:     if (edge.relation !== 'CITES') continue;
146:     if (!citedSources.has(edge.sourceId)) citedSources.set(edge.sourceId, []);
147:     citedSources.get(edge.sourceId)!.push(edge.targetId);
148:   }
149: 
150:   return citedSources;
151: }
152: 
153: /**
154:  * Compute longest-path depth for each node from any root node.
155:  * Mirrors the in-memory CJS implementation so both layers report
156:  * the same structural depth for DAG-shaped coverage graphs.
157:  */
158: function computeDepths(nodes: CoverageNode[], edges: CoverageEdge[]): Map<string, number> {
159:   const adjacency = new Map<string, string[]>();
160:   const inDegree = new Map<string, number>();
161: 
162:   for (const node of nodes) {
163:     adjacency.set(node.id, []);
164:     inDegree.set(node.id, 0);
165:   }
166: 
167:   for (const edge of edges) {
168:     if (!adjacency.has(edge.sourceId)) adjacency.set(edge.sourceId, []);
169:     adjacency.get(edge.sourceId)!.push(edge.targetId);
170:     inDegree.set(edge.targetId, (inDegree.get(edge.targetId) ?? 0) + 1);
171:     if (!inDegree.has(edge.sourceId)) inDegree.set(edge.sourceId, 0);
172:   }
173: 
174:   const depthMap = new Map<string, number>();
175:   const remaining = new Map(inDegree);
176:   const queue: string[] = [];
177: 
178:   for (const [id, degree] of remaining) {
179:     if (degree === 0) {
180:       depthMap.set(id, 0);
181:       queue.push(id);
182:     }
183:   }
184: 
185:   let queueIndex = 0;
186:   while (queueIndex < queue.length) {
187:     const current = queue[queueIndex++];
188:     const currentDepth = depthMap.get(current) ?? 0;
189: 
190:     for (const childId of adjacency.get(current) ?? []) {
191:       const candidateDepth = currentDepth + 1;
192:       if (candidateDepth > (depthMap.get(childId) ?? 0)) {
193:         depthMap.set(childId, candidateDepth);
194:       }
195: 
196:       const nextDegree = (remaining.get(childId) ?? 0) - 1;
197:       remaining.set(childId, nextDegree);
198:       if (nextDegree === 0) {
199:         queue.push(childId);
200:       }
201:     }
202:   }
203: 
204:   for (const node of nodes) {
205:     if (!depthMap.has(node.id)) depthMap.set(node.id, 0);
206:   }
207: 
208:   return depthMap;
209: }
210: 
211: function computeHotspotSaturation(d: Database.Database, ns: Namespace): number {
212:   const nodeNamespace = buildNamespacePredicate('', ns);
213:   const edgeNamespace = buildNamespacePredicate('e', ns);
214:   const files = d.prepare(`
215:     SELECT id, metadata FROM coverage_nodes
216:     WHERE ${nodeNamespace.clause} AND kind = 'FILE'
217:   `).all(...nodeNamespace.params) as Array<{ id: string; metadata: string | null }>;
218: 
219:   const hotspotFiles: string[] = [];
220:   for (const f of files) {
221:     if (f.metadata) {
222:       try {
223:         const meta = JSON.parse(f.metadata);
224:         if (meta.hotspot_score && meta.hotspot_score > 0) {
225:           hotspotFiles.push(f.id);
226:         }
227:       } catch { }
228:     }
229:   }
230: 
231:   if (hotspotFiles.length === 0) return 1.0;
232: 
233:   let saturated = 0;
234:   for (const fileId of hotspotFiles) {
235:     const dimCoverage = (d.prepare(`
236:       SELECT COUNT(DISTINCT e.source_id) as c
237:       FROM coverage_edges e
238:       JOIN coverage_nodes n ON ${buildCompositeNodeJoin('n', 'e', 'source_id')}
239:       WHERE ${edgeNamespace.clause}
240:         AND e.target_id = ?
241:         AND e.relation = 'COVERS'
242:         AND n.kind = 'DIMENSION'
243:     `).get(...edgeNamespace.params, fileId) as { c: number }).c;
244: 
245:     if (dimCoverage >= 2) saturated++;
246:   }
247: 
248:   return hotspotFiles.length > 0 ? saturated / hotspotFiles.length : 1.0;
249: }
250: 
251: // ───── CORE LOGIC ─────
252: 
253: /**
254:  * Compute degree, depth, and weight signals for all nodes in a namespace.
255:  *
256:  * @param ns - Namespace identifying the coverage graph.
257:  * @returns Array of node-level signal objects.
258:  */
259: export function computeNodeSignals(ns: Namespace): NodeSignal[] {
260:   const nodes = getNodes(ns);
261:   const edges = getEdges(ns);
262: 
263:   const inDegreeMap = new Map<string, number>();
264:   const outDegreeMap = new Map<string, number>();
265:   const weightSumMap = new Map<string, number>();
266: 
267:   for (const edge of edges) {
268:     outDegreeMap.set(edge.sourceId, (outDegreeMap.get(edge.sourceId) ?? 0) + 1);
269:     inDegreeMap.set(edge.targetId, (inDegreeMap.get(edge.targetId) ?? 0) + 1);
270:     weightSumMap.set(edge.sourceId, (weightSumMap.get(edge.sourceId) ?? 0) + edge.weight);
271:     weightSumMap.set(edge.targetId, (weightSumMap.get(edge.targetId) ?? 0) + edge.weight);
272:   }
273: 
274:   const depthMap = computeDepths(nodes, edges);
275: 
276:   return nodes.map(node => ({
277:     nodeId: node.id,
278:     kind: node.kind,
279:     degree: (inDegreeMap.get(node.id) ?? 0) + (outDegreeMap.get(node.id) ?? 0),
280:     inDegree: inDegreeMap.get(node.id) ?? 0,
281:     outDegree: outDegreeMap.get(node.id) ?? 0,
282:     depth: depthMap.get(node.id) ?? 0,
283:     weightSum: weightSumMap.get(node.id) ?? 0,
284:   }));
285: }
286: 
287: /**
288:  * Canonical research question coverage: questions with at least two ANSWERS
289:  * edges divided by all research questions in the graph.
290:  *
291:  * @param nodes - Research nodes in the graph.
292:  * @param edges - Research edges in the graph.
293:  * @returns Coverage ratio in [0, 1].
294:  */
295: export function computeResearchQuestionCoverageFromData(
296:   nodes: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalNodeLike>,
297:   edges: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalEdgeLike>,
298: ): number {
299:   const questionIds = nodes
300:     .filter(node => node.kind === 'QUESTION')
301:     .map(node => node.id);
302: 
303:   if (questionIds.length === 0) return 0;
304: 
305:   const answeringFindings = buildAnsweringFindingsByQuestion(edges);
306:   let coveredQuestions = 0;
307: 
308:   for (const questionId of questionIds) {
309:     if ((answeringFindings.get(questionId) ?? []).length >= 2) {
310:       coveredQuestions++;
311:     }
312:   }
313: 
314:   return coveredQuestions / questionIds.length;
315: }
316:

318:  * Canonical research claim verification rate: verified claims divided by all
319:  * claim nodes, where verified means verification_status exists and is not

289:   return `Convergence signals not yet met: ${trace.filter((entry) => !entry.passed).map((entry) => entry.signal).join(', ')}. Continue iterating.`;

293: // 5. CORE LOGIC
294: // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

296: async function main() {

337:       edgesByRelation: edges.reduce((acc, edge) => ({ ...acc, [edge.relation]: (acc[edge.relation] || 0) + 1 }), {}),
338:       lastIteration: snapshots.length > 0 ? snapshots[snapshots.length - 1].iteration : null,

341:     if (stats.totalNodes === 0) {

1: // MODULE: Council Graph Database
2: //
3: // Runtime-owned SQLite projection for AI Council graph state. This is a
4: // derived index: packet-local ai-council artifacts remain source-of-truth.
5: 
6: import Database from 'better-sqlite3';
7: import { mkdirSync, statSync } from 'node:fs';
8: import { dirname, join } from 'node:path';
9: import { fileURLToPath } from 'node:url';
10: 
11: // ───── TYPE DEFINITIONS ─────
12: 
13: export type CouncilNodeKind =
14:   | 'SESSION'
15:   | 'ROUND'
16:   | 'SEAT'
17:   | 'CLAIM'
18:   | 'EVIDENCE'
19:   | 'DISAGREEMENT'
20:   | 'DECISION'
21:   | 'RECOMMENDATION';
22: 
23: export type CouncilRelation =
24:   | 'PARTICIPATES_IN'
25:   | 'PROPOSES'
26:   | 'SUPPORTS'
27:   | 'CONTRADICTS'
28:   | 'DERIVES_FROM'
29:   | 'AGREES_WITH'
30:   | 'RESOLVES'
31:   | 'ESCALATES'
32:   | 'EVIDENCE_FOR'
33:   | 'RECOMMENDS';
34: 
35: export interface CouncilNode {
36:   id: string;
37:   specFolder: string;
38:   sessionId: string;
39:   kind: CouncilNodeKind;
40:   name: string;
41:   artifactPath?: string;
42:   contentHash?: string;
43:   roundId?: string;
44:   metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
45:   createdAt?: string;
46:   updatedAt?: string;
47: }
48: 
49: export interface CouncilEdge {
50:   id: string;
51:   specFolder: string;
52:   sessionId: string;
53:   sourceId: string;
54:   targetId: string;
55:   relation: CouncilRelation;
56:   weight: number;
57:   artifactPath?: string;
58:   metadata?: Record<string, unknown>;
59:   createdAt?: string;
60: }
61: 
62: export interface CouncilSnapshot {
63:   id?: number;
64:   specFolder: string;
65:   sessionId: string;
66:   roundId?: string;
67:   metrics: Record<string, unknown>;
68:   nodeCount: number;
69:   edgeCount: number;
70:   createdAt?: string;
71: }
72: 
73: export interface CouncilNamespace {
74:   specFolder: string;
75:   sessionId?: string;
76: }
77: 
78: // ───── CONSTANTS ─────
79: 
80: export const SCHEMA_VERSION = 1;
81: export const DB_FILENAME = 'council-graph.sqlite';
82: export const COUNCIL_GRAPH_STORAGE_DIR = join(
83:   dirname(fileURLToPath(import.meta.url)),
84:   '..',
85:   '..',
86:   'database',
87: );
88: 
89: const MIN_WEIGHT = 0.0;
90: const MAX_WEIGHT = 2.0;
91: 
92: export const VALID_KINDS = [
93:   'SESSION',
94:   'ROUND',
95:   'SEAT',
96:   'CLAIM',
97:   'EVIDENCE',
98:   'DISAGREEMENT',
99:   'DECISION',
100:   'RECOMMENDATION',
101: ] as const;
102: 
103: export const VALID_RELATIONS = [
104:   'PARTICIPATES_IN',
105:   'PROPOSES',
106:   'SUPPORTS',
107:   'CONTRADICTS',
108:   'DERIVES_FROM',
109:   'AGREES_WITH',
110:   'RESOLVES',
111:   'ESCALATES',
112:   'EVIDENCE_FOR',
113:   'RECOMMENDS',
114: ] as const;
115: 
116: const KIND_CHECK = VALID_KINDS.map((kind) => `'${kind}'`).join(', ');
117: const RELATION_CHECK = VALID_RELATIONS.map((relation) => `'${relation}'`).join(', ');
118: 
119: const SCHEMA_SQL = `
120:   CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS council_nodes (
121:     spec_folder TEXT NOT NULL,
122:     session_id TEXT NOT NULL,
123:     id TEXT NOT NULL,
124:     kind TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(kind IN (${KIND_CHECK})),
125:     name TEXT NOT NULL,
126:     artifact_path TEXT,
127:     content_hash TEXT,
128:     round_id TEXT,
129:     metadata TEXT,
130:     created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
131:     updated_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
132:     PRIMARY KEY (spec_folder, session_id, id)
133:   );
134: 
135:   CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS council_edges (
136:     spec_folder TEXT NOT NULL,
137:     session_id TEXT NOT NULL,
138:     id TEXT NOT NULL,
139:     source_id TEXT NOT NULL,
140:     target_id TEXT NOT NULL,
141:     relation TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(relation IN (${RELATION_CHECK})),
142:     weight REAL DEFAULT 1.0 CHECK(weight >= 0.0 AND weight <= 2.0),
143:     artifact_path TEXT,
144:     metadata TEXT,
145:     created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
146:     CHECK(source_id != target_id),
147:     PRIMARY KEY (spec_folder, session_id, id),
148:     FOREIGN KEY (spec_folder, session_id, source_id)
149:       REFERENCES council_nodes (spec_folder, session_id, id),
150:     FOREIGN KEY (spec_folder, session_id, target_id)
151:       REFERENCES council_nodes (spec_folder, session_id, id)
152:   );
153: 
154:   CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS council_snapshots (
155:     id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
156:     spec_folder TEXT NOT NULL,
157:     session_id TEXT NOT NULL,
158:     round_id TEXT,
159:     metrics TEXT,
160:     node_count INTEGER,
161:     edge_count INTEGER,
162:     created_at TEXT DEFAULT (datetime('now')),
163:     UNIQUE(spec_folder, session_id, round_id)
164:   );
165: 
166:   CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS council_schema_version (
167:     version INTEGER NOT NULL
168:   );
169: 
170:   CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_council_nodes_session ON council_nodes(spec_folder, session_id);
171:   CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_council_nodes_kind ON council_nodes(kind);
172:   CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_council_nodes_round ON council_nodes(spec_folder, session_id, round_id);
173:   CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_council_edges_source ON council_edges(spec_folder, session_id, source_id);
174:   CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_council_edges_target ON council_edges(spec_folder, session_id, target_id);
175:   CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_council_edges_relation ON council_edges(relation);
176:   CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS idx_council_snapshots_session ON council_snapshots(spec_folder, session_id);
177: `;
178: 
179: // ───── DATABASE LIFECYCLE ─────
180: 
181: let db: Database.Database | null = null;
182: let dbPath: string | null = null;
183: let statementDb: Database.Database | null = null;
184: const preparedStatements = new Map<string, Database.Statement>();
185: 
186: /**
187:  * Initialize (or get) the council graph database.
188:  *
189:  * Creates the runtime database directory and file, applies the schema, and
190:  * enables WAL journaling and foreign keys.
191:  *
192:  * @param dbDir - Directory for the database file.
193:  * @returns The initialized Database instance.
194:  */
195: export function initDb(dbDir: string = COUNCIL_GRAPH_STORAGE_DIR): Database.Database {
196:   if (db) return db;
197: 
198:   try {
199:     mkdirSync(dbDir, { recursive: true });
200:     dbPath = join(dbDir, DB_FILENAME);
201:     db = new Database(dbPath);
202:     db.pragma('journal_mode = WAL');
203:     db.pragma('foreign_keys = ON');
204:     db.exec(SCHEMA_SQL);
205: 
206:     const versionRow = db.prepare('SELECT version FROM council_schema_version LIMIT 1').get() as { version: number } | undefined;
207:     if (!versionRow) {
208:       db.prepare('INSERT INTO council_schema_version (version) VALUES (?)').run(SCHEMA_VERSION);
209:     } else if (versionRow.version < SCHEMA_VERSION) {
210:       db.prepare('UPDATE council_schema_version SET version = ?').run(SCHEMA_VERSION);
211:     }
212: 
213:     return db;
214:   } catch (err: unknown) {
215:     if (db) {
216:       try { db.close(); } catch { }
217:     }
218:     db = null;
219:     dbPath = null;
220:     statementDb = null;

13: # Deep Loop Runtime Script Interface Contract
14: 
15: Formal contract for the direct `.cjs` script entry points under `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/`.
16: 
17: ---
18: 
19: ## 1. OVERVIEW
20: 
21: The 118 FULL_ISOLATE_NO_MCP arc removed the four `mcp__mk_spec_memory__deep_loop_graph_*` tools. Their live replacement is direct process invocation through four `.cjs` scripts.
22: 
23: | Script | Replaces | Purpose |
24: |---|---|---|
25: | `scripts/convergence.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_convergence` | Computes graph-aware continue or stop decisions. |
26: | `scripts/upsert.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_upsert` | Stores graph nodes and edges. |
27: | `scripts/query.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_query` | Reads graph views. |
28: | `scripts/status.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_status` | Reports graph health. |
29: 
30: Every script follows the same lifecycle: parse CLI args, re-exec through the TSX loader when needed, import the TypeScript library, open SQLite through `coverage-graph-db.ts`, execute, emit one JSON object to stdout, close DB in `finally`, and map failures to exit codes.
31: 
32: ---
33: 
34: ## 2. SHARED PROCESS CONTRACT
35: 
36: ### Loader Contract
37: 
38: - Scripts are Node executables with `#!/usr/bin/env node`.
39: - Line 2 is `'use strict';`.
40: - Scripts resolve `system-spec-kit/scripts/node_modules/tsx/dist/loader.mjs`.
41: - If `DEEP_LOOP_TSX_LOADED !== '1'`, the script spawns Node with `--import <tsx-loader>` and original args.
42: - The child receives cwd, env plus `DEEP_LOOP_TSX_LOADED=1`, and stdin when stdin is not a TTY.
43: 
44: ### Stdout Contract
45: 
46: - Stdout is JSON-only for workflow-facing responses.
47: - Success uses `status: "ok"`.
48: - Failure uses `status: "error"`, `error`, and `code`.
49: - Script errors may write diagnostic stack JSON to stderr.
50: - Workflow YAML should parse stdout, not stderr.
51: 
52: ### Exit-Code Matrix
53: 
54: | Exit code | Meaning | Source condition |
55: |---:|---|---|
56: | 0 | OK | Valid input and successful operation. |
57: | 1 | Script error | Unexpected runtime failure or uncategorized exception. |
58: | 2 | DB error | SQLite or DB lifecycle failure. |
59: | 3 | Input validation error | Missing args, invalid loop type, invalid query type, malformed JSON, or unexpected positional args. |
60: 
61: ### Required Namespace Args
62: 
63: | Arg | Required | Values | Notes |
64: |---|---|---|---|
65: | `--spec-folder` | Yes | path string | Stored as `specFolder`. |
66: | `--loop-type` | Yes | `research` or `review` | Validated before DB operations. |
67: | `--session-id` | Yes | string | Scopes graph reads and writes to one loop lineage. |
68: 
69: ---
70: 
71: ## 3. SCRIPT CONTRACTS
72: 
73: ### convergence.cjs
74: 
75: Source: `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/convergence.cjs`
76: 
77: Additional args: `--iteration`, `--persist-snapshot`.
78: 
79: Workflow-facing fields: `graph_decision`, `graph_signals_json`, `graph_blockers_json`, `graph_stop_blocked`, `graph_convergence_score`.
80: 
81: Behavior: Returns `CONTINUE` for empty graphs, `STOP_BLOCKED` when blocking guards fail, and `STOP_ALLOWED` when all convergence signals pass.
82: 
83: ### upsert.cjs
84: 
85: Source: `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/upsert.cjs`
86: 
87: Additional args: `--nodes`, `--edges`, or `--events`.
88: 
89: Workflow-facing fields: `graph_nodes_json`, `graph_edges_json`, `graph_upsert_event_count`.
90: 
91: Behavior: Requires at least one node or edge, validates kinds and relations by loop type, rejects self-loops, and clamps weights.
92: 
93: ### query.cjs
94: 
95: Source: `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/query.cjs`
96: 
97: Additional args: `--query-type`, optional `--limit`, `--node-id`, `--max-depth`.
98: 
99: Workflow-facing fields: `data.queryType`, `data.namespace`, query-specific arrays.
100: 
101: Behavior: Supports uncovered questions, coverage gaps, unverified claims, contradictions, provenance chains, and hot nodes.
102: 
103: ### status.cjs
104: 
105: Source: `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/status.cjs`
106: 
107: Additional args: none beyond namespace args.
108: 
109: Workflow-facing fields: `schemaVersion`, `rowCount`, counts, breakdowns, signals.
110: 
111: Behavior: Reports clean empty state with null signals when no nodes exist.
112: 
113: ---
114: 
115: ## 4. DB LIFECYCLE REQUIREMENT
116: 
117: Scripts must not open SQLite directly. They import `lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts`, rely on lazy `getDb()` or library functions, and call `db.closeDb()` inside `finally`.
118: 
119: Visible anchors:
120: 
121: - `scripts/convergence.cjs` closes after signal and query work.
122: - `scripts/upsert.cjs` closes after `batchUpsert`.
123: - `scripts/query.cjs` closes after query dispatch.
124: - `scripts/status.cjs` closes after status assembly.
125: - `tests/lifecycle/db-open-close.vitest.ts` covers lifecycle behavior.
126: 
127: ---
128: 
129: ## 5. PERMISSIONS-GATE RELATIONSHIP
130: 
131: `permissions-gate` is not called directly by the scripts. It is part of the pre-dispatch runtime safety layer for loop orchestration. Script execution is an `execute` operation that should be allowed only for runtime-owned script paths in any active permission matrix.
132: 
133: Relevant source:
134: 
135: - `lib/deep-loop/permissions-gate.ts` maps tools and bash commands to operation classes.
136: - `SKILL.md` states direct `.cjs` invocation is the supported consumer path.
137: - `/doctor deep-loop` marks the DB route as mutating before snapshots or repair.
138: 
139: ---
140: 
141: ## 6. FAILURE TRIAGE
142: 
143: | Symptom | First check | Likely fix |
144: |---|---|---|
145: | Exit 3 | Missing namespace arg or malformed JSON | Correct CLI args or JSON quoting. |
146: | Exit 2 | SQLite open or query failure | Inspect schema version and DB path. |
147: | Exit 1 | Unexpected exception | Read stderr stack JSON, then inspect the called library. |
148: | Non-JSON stdout | Script contract drift | Restore JSON-only stdout or update YAML output bindings. |
149: | Empty convergence output | Graph has zero nodes | Upsert graph events before convergence or accept `CONTINUE`. |
150: 
151: ---
152: 
153: ## 7. SOURCE ANCHORS
154: 
155: | Path | Contract Role |
156: |---|---|
157: | `scripts/convergence.cjs` | Stop-decision script and convergence output bindings. |
158: | `scripts/upsert.cjs` | Mutation script and graph event ingestion. |
159: | `scripts/query.cjs` | Read script and query-type dispatch. |
160: | `scripts/status.cjs` | Health script and schema/count reporting. |
161: | `lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts` | DB lifecycle owner. |
162: | `lib/deep-loop/permissions-gate.ts` | Pre-dispatch execute/write boundary. |
163: | `tests/integration/*-script.vitest.ts` | Script-level regression coverage. |
164: | `tests/lifecycle/db-open-close.vitest.ts` | DB lifecycle regression coverage. |
165: 

(End of file - total 165 lines)

# Deep Loop Runtime Script Interface Contract

Formal contract for the direct `.cjs` script entry points under `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/`.

---

## 1. OVERVIEW

The 118 FULL_ISOLATE_NO_MCP arc removed the four `mcp__mk_spec_memory__deep_loop_graph_*` tools. Their live replacement is direct process invocation through four `.cjs` scripts.

| Script | Replaces | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `scripts/convergence.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_convergence` | Computes graph-aware continue or stop decisions. |
| `scripts/upsert.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_upsert` | Stores graph nodes and edges. |
| `scripts/query.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_query` | Reads graph views. |
| `scripts/status.cjs` | `deep_loop_graph_status` | Reports graph health. |

Every script follows the same lifecycle: parse CLI args, re-exec through the TSX loader when needed, import the TypeScript library, open SQLite through `coverage-graph-db.ts`, execute, emit one JSON object to stdout, close DB in `finally`, and map failures to exit codes.

---

## 2. SHARED PROCESS CONTRACT

### Loader Contract

- Scripts are Node executables with `#!/usr/bin/env node`.
- Line 2 is `'use strict';`.
- Scripts resolve `system-spec-kit/scripts/node_modules/tsx/dist/loader.mjs`.
- If `DEEP_LOOP_TSX_LOADED !== '1'`, the script spawns Node with `--import <tsx-loader>` and original args.
- The child receives cwd, env plus `DEEP_LOOP_TSX_LOADED=1`, and stdin when stdin is not a TTY.

### Stdout Contract

- Stdout is JSON-only for workflow-facing responses.
- Success uses `status: "ok"`.
- Failure uses `status: "error"`, `error`, and `code`.
- Script errors may write diagnostic stack JSON to stderr.
- Workflow YAML should parse stdout, not stderr.

### Exit-Code Matrix

| Exit code | Meaning | Source condition |
|---:|---|---|
| 0 | OK | Valid input and successful operation. |
| 1 | Script error | Unexpected runtime failure or uncategorized exception. |
| 2 | DB error | SQLite or DB lifecycle failure. |
| 3 | Input validation error | Missing args, invalid loop type, invalid query type, malformed JSON, or unexpected positional args. |

### Required Namespace Args

| Arg | Required | Values | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| `--spec-folder` | Yes | path string | Stored as `specFolder`. |
| `--loop-type` | Yes | `research` or `review` | Validated before DB operations. |
| `--session-id` | Yes | string | Scopes graph reads and writes to one loop lineage. |

---

## 3. SCRIPT CONTRACTS

### convergence.cjs

Source: `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/convergence.cjs`

Additional args: `--iteration`, `--persist-snapshot`.

Workflow-facing fields: `graph_decision`, `graph_signals_json`, `graph_blockers_json`, `graph_stop_blocked`, `graph_convergence_score`.

Behavior: Returns `CONTINUE` for empty graphs, `STOP_BLOCKED` when blocking guards fail, and `STOP_ALLOWED` when all convergence signals pass.

### upsert.cjs

Source: `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/upsert.cjs`

Additional args: `--nodes`, `--edges`, or `--events`.

Workflow-facing fields: `graph_nodes_json`, `graph_edges_json`, `graph_upsert_event_count`.

Behavior: Requires at least one node or edge, validates kinds and relations by loop type, rejects self-loops, and clamps weights.

### query.cjs

Source: `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/query.cjs`

Additional args: `--query-type`, optional `--limit`, `--node-id`, `--max-depth`.

Workflow-facing fields: `data.queryType`, `data.namespace`, query-specific arrays.

Behavior: Supports uncovered questions, coverage gaps, unverified claims, contradictions, provenance chains, and hot nodes.

### status.cjs

Source: `.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/scripts/status.cjs`

Additional args: none beyond namespace args.

Workflow-facing fields: `schemaVersion`, `rowCount`, counts, breakdowns, signals.

Behavior: Reports clean empty state with null signals when no nodes exist.

---

## 4. DB LIFECYCLE REQUIREMENT

Scripts must not open SQLite directly. They import `lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts`, rely on lazy `getDb()` or library functions, and call `db.closeDb()` inside `finally`.

Visible anchors:

- `scripts/convergence.cjs` closes after signal and query work.
- `scripts/upsert.cjs` closes after `batchUpsert`.
- `scripts/query.cjs` closes after query dispatch.
- `scripts/status.cjs` closes after status assembly.
- `tests/lifecycle/db-open-close.vitest.ts` covers lifecycle behavior.

---

## 5. PERMISSIONS-GATE RELATIONSHIP

`permissions-gate` is not called directly by the scripts. It is part of the pre-dispatch runtime safety layer for loop orchestration. Script execution is an `execute` operation that should be allowed only for runtime-owned script paths in any active permission matrix.

Relevant source:

- `lib/deep-loop/permissions-gate.ts` maps tools and bash commands to operation classes.
- `SKILL.md` states direct `.cjs` invocation is the supported consumer path.
- `/doctor deep-loop` marks the DB route as mutating before snapshots or repair.

---

## 6. FAILURE TRIAGE

| Symptom | First check | Likely fix |
|---|---|---|
| Exit 3 | Missing namespace arg or malformed JSON | Correct CLI args or JSON quoting. |
| Exit 2 | SQLite open or query failure | Inspect schema version and DB path. |
| Exit 1 | Unexpected exception | Read stderr stack JSON, then inspect the called library. |
| Non-JSON stdout | Script contract drift | Restore JSON-only stdout or update YAML output bindings. |
| Empty convergence output | Graph has zero nodes | Upsert graph events before convergence or accept `CONTINUE`. |

---

## 7. SOURCE ANCHORS

| Path | Contract Role |
|---|---|
| `scripts/convergence.cjs` | Stop-decision script and convergence output bindings. |
| `scripts/upsert.cjs` | Mutation script and graph event ingestion. |
| `scripts/query.cjs` | Read script and query-type dispatch. |
| `scripts/status.cjs` | Health script and schema/count reporting. |
| `lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-db.ts` | DB lifecycle owner. |
| `lib/deep-loop/permissions-gate.ts` | Pre-dispatch execute/write boundary. |
| `tests/integration/*-script.vitest.ts` | Script-level regression coverage. |
| `tests/lifecycle/db-open-close.vitest.ts` | DB lifecycle regression coverage. |

<path>/Users/michelkerkmeester/MEGA/Development/Code_Environment/Public/.opencode/skills/deep-loop-runtime/lib/coverage-graph/coverage-graph-signals.ts</path>
<type>file</type>
<content>
321:  *
322:  * Zero claim nodes is a vacuous pass (returns 1.0): there is nothing to verify,
323:  * so this signal must not block convergence. Returning 0 would mark the trace
324:  * entry failed and force a claimless-but-otherwise-converged graph to loop
325:  * forever, since no unverified-claims blocker is raised for the empty case.
326:  * This mirrors p0ResolutionRate, which also returns 1.0 when its denominator
327:  * is zero.
328:  *
329:  * @param nodes - Research nodes in the graph.
330:  * @returns Verification rate in [0, 1]; 1.0 when there are no claim nodes.
331:  */
332: export function computeResearchClaimVerificationRateFromData(
333:   nodes: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalNodeLike>,
334: ): number {
335:   const claims = nodes.filter(node => node.kind === 'CLAIM');
336:   if (claims.length === 0) return 1.0;
337: 
338:   let verifiedClaims = 0;
339:   for (const claim of claims) {
340:     const meta = parseNodeMetadata(claim.metadata);
341:     if (meta?.verification_status && meta.verification_status !== 'unresolved') {
342:       verifiedClaims++;
343:     }
344:   }
345: 
346:   return verifiedClaims / claims.length;
347: }
348: 
349: /**
350:  * Canonical research contradiction density: CONTRADICTS edges divided by all
351:  * research edges in the graph.
352:  *
353:  * @param edges - Research edges in the graph.
354:  * @returns Contradiction density in [0, 1].
355:  */
356: export function computeResearchContradictionDensityFromData(
357:   edges: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalEdgeLike>,
358: ): number {
359:   if (edges.length === 0) return 0;
360:   let contradictionCount = 0;
361: 
362:   for (const edge of edges) {
363:     if (edge.relation === 'CONTRADICTS') contradictionCount++;
364:   }
365: 
366:   return contradictionCount / edges.length;
367: }
368: 
369: /**
370:  * Canonical research source diversity: for each question, count distinct
371:  * source metadata quality classes reachable through ANSWERS -> CITES paths,
372:  * then average that count across all questions.
373:  *
374:  * @param nodes - Research nodes in the graph.
375:  * @param edges - Research edges in the graph.
376:  * @returns Average source diversity score.
377:  */
378: export function computeResearchSourceDiversityFromData(
379:   nodes: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalNodeLike>,
380:   edges: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalEdgeLike>,
381: ): number {
382:   const questionIds = nodes
383:     .filter(node => node.kind === 'QUESTION')
384:     .map(node => node.id);
385: 
386:   if (questionIds.length === 0) return 0;
387: 
388:   const answeringFindings = buildAnsweringFindingsByQuestion(edges);
389:   const citedSources = buildCitedSourcesByFinding(edges);
390:   const sourceMetadataById = new Map<string, Record<string, unknown>>();
391: 
392:   for (const node of nodes) {
393:     if (node.kind !== 'SOURCE') continue;
394:     const meta = parseNodeMetadata(node.metadata);
395:     if (meta) sourceMetadataById.set(node.id, meta);
396:   }
397: 
398:   let totalDiversity = 0;
399: 
400:   for (const questionId of questionIds) {
401:     const qualityClasses = new Set<string>();
402: 
403:     for (const findingId of answeringFindings.get(questionId) ?? []) {
404:       for (const sourceId of citedSources.get(findingId) ?? []) {
405:         const qualityClass = sourceMetadataById.get(sourceId)?.quality_class;
406:         if (typeof qualityClass === 'string' && qualityClass.length > 0) {
407:           qualityClasses.add(qualityClass);
408:         }
409:       }
410:     }
411: 
412:     totalDiversity += qualityClasses.size;
413:   }
414: 
415:   return totalDiversity / questionIds.length;
416: }
417: 
418: /**
419:  * Canonical research evidence depth: average path length across all
420:  * question -> finding paths, scoring 2 when the finding cites at least one
421:  * source and 1 when it does not.
422:  *
423:  * @param nodes - Research nodes in the graph.
424:  * @param edges - Research edges in the graph.
425:  * @returns Average evidence depth score.
426:  */
427: export function computeResearchEvidenceDepthFromData(
428:   nodes: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalNodeLike>,
429:   edges: ReadonlyArray<ResearchSignalEdgeLike>,
430: ): number {
431:   const questionIds = nodes
432:     .filter(node => node.kind === 'QUESTION')
433:     .map(node => node.id);
434: 
435:   if (questionIds.length === 0) return 0;
436: 
437:   const answeringFindings = buildAnsweringFindingsByQuestion(edges);
438:   const citedSources = buildCitedSourcesByFinding(edges);
439:   let totalDepth = 0;
440:   let pathCount = 0;
441: 
442:   for (const questionId of questionIds) {
443:     for (const findingId of answeringFindings.get(questionId) ?? []) {
444:       totalDepth += (citedSources.get(findingId) ?? []).length > 0 ? 2 : 1;
445:       pathCount++;
446:     }
447:   }
448: 
449:   return pathCount > 0 ? totalDepth / pathCount : 0;
450: }
451: 
452: /**
453:  * Compute research convergence signals from raw node and edge data.
454:  *
455:  * @param ns - Namespace identifying the coverage graph.
456:  * @returns Research convergence signals with all five metrics.
457:  */
458: export function computeResearchSignals(ns: Namespace): ResearchConvergenceSignals {
459:   const nodes = getNodes(ns);
460:   const edges = getEdges(ns);
461: 
462:   const questionCoverage = computeResearchQuestionCoverageFromData(nodes, edges);
463:   const claimVerificationRate = computeResearchClaimVerificationRateFromData(nodes);
464:   const contradictionDensity = computeResearchContradictionDensityFromData(edges);
465:   const sourceDiversity = computeResearchSourceDiversityFromData(nodes, edges);
466:   const evidenceDepth = computeResearchEvidenceDepthFromData(nodes, edges);
467: 
468:   return {
469:     questionCoverage,
470:     claimVerificationRate,
471:     contradictionDensity,
472:     sourceDiversity,
473:     evidenceDepth,
474:   };
475: }
476: 
477: /**
478:  * Compute review convergence signals using SQL aggregation.
479:  *
480:  * @param ns - Namespace identifying the coverage graph.
481:  * @returns Review convergence signals with all five metrics.
482:  */
483: export function computeReviewSignals(ns: Namespace): ReviewConvergenceSignals {
484:   const d = getDb();
485:   const nodeNamespace = buildNamespacePredicate('', ns);
486:   const scopedNodeNamespace = buildNamespacePredicate('n', ns);
487:   const edgeNamespace = buildNamespacePredicate('e', ns);
488:   const bareEdgeNamespace = buildNamespacePredicate('', ns);
489: 
490:   const allDimensions = (d.prepare(
491:     `SELECT COUNT(*) as c FROM coverage_nodes WHERE ${nodeNamespace.clause} AND kind = 'DIMENSION'`,
492:   ).get(...nodeNamespace.params) as { c: number }).c;
493: 
494:   const coveredDimensions = (d.prepare(`
495:     SELECT COUNT(*) as c FROM coverage_nodes n
496:     WHERE ${scopedNodeNamespace.clause} AND n.kind = 'DIMENSION'
497:       AND EXISTS (
498:         SELECT 1 FROM coverage_edges e
499:         WHERE ${edgeNamespace.clause}
500:           AND e.source_id = n.id
501:           AND e.relation = 'COVERS'
502:       )
503:   `).get(...scopedNodeNamespace.params, ...edgeNamespace.params) as { c: number }).c;
504: 
505:   const dimensionCoverage = allDimensions > 0 ? coveredDimensions / allDimensions : 0;
506: 
507:   const allFindings = (d.prepare(
508:     `SELECT COUNT(*) as c FROM coverage_nodes WHERE ${nodeNamespace.clause} AND kind = 'FINDING'`,
509:   ).get(...nodeNamespace.params) as { c: number }).c;
510: 
511:   const stableFindings = (d.prepare(`
512:     SELECT COUNT(*) as c FROM coverage_nodes n
513:     WHERE ${scopedNodeNamespace.clause} AND n.kind = 'FINDING'
514:       AND NOT EXISTS (
515:         SELECT 1 FROM coverage_edges e
516:         WHERE ${edgeNamespace.clause}
517:           AND (e.source_id = n.id OR e.target_id = n.id)
518:           AND e.relation = 'CONTRADICTS'
519:       )
520:   `).get(...scopedNodeNamespace.params, ...edgeNamespace.params) as { c: number }).c;
521: 
522:   const findingStability = allFindings > 0 ? stableFindings / allFindings : 0;
523: 
524:   const allP0 = d.prepare(`
525:     SELECT id, metadata FROM coverage_nodes
526:     WHERE ${nodeNamespace.clause} AND kind = 'FINDING'
527:   `).all(...nodeNamespace.params) as Array<{ id: string; metadata: string | null }>;
528: 
529:   let p0Count = 0;
530:   let p0Resolved = 0;
531: 
532:   for (const finding of allP0) {
533:     if (finding.metadata) {
534:       try {
535:         const meta = JSON.parse(finding.metadata);
536:         if (meta.severity === 'P0') {
537:           p0Count++;
538:           const hasResolve = (d.prepare(
539:             `SELECT COUNT(*) as c FROM coverage_edges e WHERE ${edgeNamespace.clause} AND e.target_id = ? AND e.relation = 'RESOLVES'`,
540:           ).get(...edgeNamespace.params, finding.id) as { c: number }).c;
541:           if (hasResolve > 0) p0Resolved++;
542:         }
543:       } catch { }
544:     }
545:   }
546: 
547:   const p0ResolutionRate = p0Count > 0 ? p0Resolved / p0Count : 1.0;
548: 
549:   const totalEvidenceEdges = (d.prepare(
550:     `SELECT COUNT(*) as c FROM coverage_edges WHERE ${bareEdgeNamespace.clause} AND relation = 'EVIDENCE_FOR'`,
551:   ).get(...bareEdgeNamespace.params) as { c: number }).c;
552: 
553:   const evidenceDensity = allFindings > 0 ? totalEvidenceEdges / allFindings : 0;
554: 
555:   const hotspotSaturation = computeHotspotSaturation(d, ns);
556: 
557:   return {
558:     dimensionCoverage,
559:     findingStability,
560:     p0ResolutionRate,
561:     evidenceDensity,
562:     hotspotSaturation,
563:   };
564: }
565: 
566: // ───── CONTEXT SIGNALS ─────
567: 
568: // Findings below this relevance are noise and excluded from "kept" coverage, so
569: // small-model over-collection cannot masquerade as new context and block saturation.
570: const CONTEXT_RELEVANCE_GATE = 0.55;
571: // By-model fan-out: a finding independently confirmed by this many executors is high-confidence.
572: const CONTEXT_AGREEMENT_MIN = 2;
573: 
574: function contextMeta(node: CoverageNode): Record<string, unknown> {
575:   return node.metadata && typeof node.metadata === 'object' ? node.metadata : {};
576: }
577: 
578: function contextAgreement(node: CoverageNode, confirmCountById: Map<string, number>): number {
579:   const metaConfirmations = Number((contextMeta(node) as { confirmations?: unknown }).confirmations);
580:   const edgeConfirmations = confirmCountById.get(node.id) ?? 0;
581:   return Math.max(Number.isFinite(metaConfirmations) ? metaConfirmations : 0, edgeConfirmations);
582: }
583: 
584: function contextRelevance(node: CoverageNode): number {
585:   const relevance = Number((contextMeta(node) as { relevance?: unknown }).relevance);
586:   return Number.isFinite(relevance) ? relevance : 0;
587: }
588: 
589: /**
590:  * Compute context convergence signals from raw node and edge data.
591:  *
592:  * Pure (no DB) so both computeContextSignals and the convergence script share
593:  * one implementation. Agreement is read from a node's CONFIRMS in-edges OR its
594:  * metadata.confirmations (whichever is higher); relevance from metadata.relevance.
595:  * Each signal vacuous-passes (1.0) when its kind is absent so a feature with no
596:  * dependencies (etc.) is not penalized — matching the review p0ResolutionRate rule.
597:  *
598:  * @param nodes - Coverage nodes for the namespace.
599:  * @param edges - Coverage edges for the namespace.
600:  * @returns Context convergence signals with all five metrics.
601:  */
602: export function computeContextSignalsFromData(
603:   nodes: CoverageNode[],
604:   edges: CoverageEdge[],
605: ): ContextConvergenceSignals {
606:   const sliceNodes = nodes.filter((node) => node.kind === 'SLICE');
607:   const reuseNodes = nodes.filter((node) => node.kind === 'REUSE_CANDIDATE');
608:   const dependencyNodes = nodes.filter((node) => node.kind === 'DEPENDENCY');
609:   const findingKinds = new Set(['REUSE_CANDIDATE', 'PATTERN', 'CONSTRAINT']);
610:   const findingNodes = nodes.filter((node) => findingKinds.has(node.kind));
611: 
612:   const coveredSliceIds = new Set(
613:     edges.filter((edge) => edge.relation === 'COVERED_BY').map((edge) => edge.sourceId),
614:   );
615:   const confirmCountById = new Map<string, number>();
616:   for (const edge of edges) {
617:     if (edge.relation === 'CONFIRMS') {
618:       confirmCountById.set(edge.targetId, (confirmCountById.get(edge.targetId) ?? 0) + 1);
619:     }
620:   }
621:   // DEPENDS_ON is SYMBOL -> DEPENDENCY (the DEPENDENCY node is the edge TARGET), so a
622:   // dependency is "resolved" when it is the target of a DEPENDS_ON edge. IMPORTS is
623:   // FILE -> FILE and never has a DEPENDENCY endpoint, so it is excluded here.
624:   const resolvedDependencyIds = new Set(
625:     edges
626:       .filter((edge) => edge.relation === 'DEPENDS_ON')
627:       .map((edge) => edge.targetId),
628:   );
629: 
630:   const sliceCoverage = sliceNodes.length > 0
631:     ? sliceNodes.filter((node) => coveredSliceIds.has(node.id)).length / sliceNodes.length
632:     : 1;
633:   const reuseCatalogCoverage = reuseNodes.length > 0
634:     ? reuseNodes.filter((node) => contextAgreement(node, confirmCountById) >= 1
635:         || (contextMeta(node) as { verified?: unknown }).verified === true).length / reuseNodes.length
636:     : 1;
637:   // agreementRate is measured over RELEVANCE-GATED findings only: below-gate noise
638:   // is not real context and must not depress cross-executor agreement. relevanceFloor
639:   // separately tracks how much of the catalog clears the gate.
640:   const gatedFindingNodes = findingNodes.filter((node) => contextRelevance(node) >= CONTEXT_RELEVANCE_GATE);
641:   const agreementRate = gatedFindingNodes.length > 0
642:     ? gatedFindingNodes.filter((node) => contextAgreement(node, confirmCountById) >= CONTEXT_AGREEMENT_MIN).length / gatedFindingNodes.length
643:     : 1;
644:   const relevanceFloor = findingNodes.length > 0
645:     ? findingNodes.filter((node) => contextRelevance(node) >= CONTEXT_RELEVANCE_GATE).length / findingNodes.length
646:     : 1;
647:   const dependencyCompleteness = dependencyNodes.length > 0
648:     ? dependencyNodes.filter((node) => resolvedDependencyIds.has(node.id)).length / dependencyNodes.length
649:     : 1;
650: 
651:   return { sliceCoverage, reuseCatalogCoverage, agreementRate, relevanceFloor, dependencyCompleteness };
652: }
653: 
654: /**
655:  * Compute context convergence signals for a namespace.
656:  *
657:  * @param ns - Namespace identifying the coverage graph.
658:  * @returns Context convergence signals with all five metrics.
659:  */
660: export function computeContextSignals(ns: Namespace): ContextConvergenceSignals {
661:   return computeContextSignalsFromData(getNodes(ns), getEdges(ns));
662: }
663: 
664: // ───── EXPORTS ─────
665: 
666: /**
667:  * Compute all convergence signals for a namespace.
668:  *
669:  * Dispatches to research or review signal computation based on loop type.
670:  *
671:  * @param ns - Namespace identifying the coverage graph.
672:  * @returns Research or review convergence signals.
673:  */
674: export function computeSignals(ns: Namespace): ConvergenceSignals {
675:   if (ns.loopType === 'research') {
676:     return computeResearchSignals(ns);
677:   }
678:   if (ns.loopType === 'context') {
679:     return computeContextSignals(ns);
680:   }
681:   return computeReviewSignals(ns);
682: }
683: 
684: /**
685:  * Create a signal snapshot for a given iteration.
686:  *
687:  * Computes convergence signals and node-level signals, then persists
688:  * them via the coverage graph database.
689:  *
690:  * @param ns - Namespace identifying the coverage graph.
691:  * @param iteration - Current iteration number.
692:  * @returns Signal snapshot with signals, node signals, and counts.
693:  */
694: export function createSignalSnapshot(ns: Namespace, iteration: number): SignalSnapshot {
695:   const signals = computeSignals(ns);
696:   const nodeSignals = computeNodeSignals(ns);
697:   const stats = getStats(ns.specFolder, ns.loopType);
698: 
699:   const snapshot: SignalSnapshot = {
700:     iteration,
701:     signals,
702:     nodeSignals,
703:     nodeCount: stats.totalNodes,
704:     edgeCount: stats.totalEdges,
705:   };
706: 
707:   createSnapshot({
708:     specFolder: ns.specFolder,
709:     loopType: ns.loopType,
710:     sessionId: ns.sessionId ?? 'legacy',
711:     iteration,
712:     metrics: {
713:       ...signals,
714:       nodeCount: stats.totalNodes,
715:       edgeCount: stats.totalEdges,
716:     },
717:     nodeCount: stats.totalNodes,
718:     edgeCount: stats.totalEdges,
719:   });
720: 
721:   return snapshot;
722: }
723: 
724: /**
725:  * Compute momentum (change rate) between the latest and previous snapshots.
726:  *
727:  * @param specFolder - Spec folder for namespace scoping.
728:  * @param loopType - Loop type ('research' or 'review').
729:  * @param sessionId - Optional session ID for scoping.
730:  * @returns Map of metric names to deltas, or null if fewer than 2 snapshots.
731:  */
732: export function computeMomentum(
733:   specFolder: string,
734:   loopType: LoopType,
735:   sessionId?: string,
736: ): Record<string, number> | null {
737:   const snapshots = getSnapshots(specFolder, loopType, sessionId);
738:   if (snapshots.length < 2) return null;
739: 
740:   const latest = snapshots[snapshots.length - 1]?.metrics ?? {};
741:   const previous = snapshots[snapshots.length - 2]?.metrics ?? {};
742: 
743:   const momentum: Record<string, number> = {};
744:   for (const key of Object.keys(latest)) {
745:     if (typeof latest[key] === 'number' && typeof previous[key] === 'number') {
746:       momentum[key] = latest[key] - previous[key];
747:     }
748:   }
749: 
750:   return momentum;
751: }

(End of file - total 751 lines)
</content>

262:   } catch {

277: 
278: /**
279:  * Acquire an exclusive writer lock via `wx` file open on the given path.
280:  * Returns a release function that closes the file descriptor and removes
281:  * the lock file. Retries briefly on contention so parallel test files and
282:  * near-simultaneous runtime callers do not fail on transient writer overlap.
283:  *
284:  * Self-heals orphaned locks: the owner pid, a creation timestamp, and a unique
285:  * per-acquisition nonce are written into the lock file. On EEXIST a lock held by
286:  * a dead pid (or one older than the max-hold age) is reclaimed (best-effort
287:  * remove, then retry). A lock held by a LIVE pid inside the age window keeps the
288:  * original maxWait/retry-deadline-then-throw behavior.
289:  *
290:  * The nonce closes two mutual-exclusion races against a concurrent reclaimer:
291:  *  - Acquire: after `wx` + stamp, the file is re-read; if it no longer carries
292:  *    THIS acquisition's nonce (a peer rmSync'd ours and recreated theirs in the
293:  *    window), the fd is closed and the acquire loop re-evaluated. The lock is
294:  *    only considered held once the on-disk body carries our nonce.
295:  *  - Release: the lock is removed only if its on-disk body still carries our
296:  *    nonce. If we were reclaimed mid-write, release skips the rmSync (but still
297:  *    closes the fd) so it never deletes the new holder's lock.
298:  *
299:  * @param {string} lockPath - Path to the lock file to create.
300:  * @returns {Function} Release function (call to unlock and clean up).
301:  * @throws {Error} With code DB_ERROR if the lock remains held after retry.
302:  */
303: function acquireWriterLock(lockPath) {
304:   fs.mkdirSync(path.dirname(lockPath), { recursive: true });
305:   const maxWaitMs = readNonNegativeEnvNumber('DEEP_LOOP_WRITER_LOCK_MAX_WAIT_MS', DEFAULT_WRITER_LOCK_MAX_WAIT_MS);
306:   const retryIntervalMs = readNonNegativeEnvNumber('DEEP_LOOP_WRITER_LOCK_RETRY_INTERVAL_MS', DEFAULT_WRITER_LOCK_RETRY_INTERVAL_MS);
307:   const maxHoldMs = readNonNegativeEnvNumber('DEEP_LOOP_WRITER_LOCK_MAX_HOLD_MS', DEFAULT_WRITER_LOCK_MAX_HOLD_MS);
308:   const deadline = Date.now() + maxWaitMs;
309:   // Cap reclaim attempts so a never-removable or re-stamped stale lock falls
310:   // through to the maxWait/throw path instead of spinning indefinitely.
311:   const MAX_RECLAIM_ATTEMPTS = 8;
312:   let reclaimAttempts = 0;
313:   // Unique per acquisition. Stamped into the lock body so a concurrent reclaimer
314:   // that rmSync's our file and recreates its own is detectable: the on-disk
315:   // nonce will no longer be ours. Drives both the acquire re-read and release.
316:   const nonce = randomUUID();
317:   let fd;
318: 
319:   while (true) {
320:     try {
321:       fd = fs.openSync(lockPath, 'wx');
322:       // Stamp owner pid + creation time + nonce so other acquirers can detect a
323:       // stale (dead-pid or aged-out) lock and so we can prove ownership. A write
324:       // failure must not forfeit a lock we legitimately hold — staleness then
325:       // falls back to mtime (still fresh). But without a stamped nonce we cannot
326:       // distinguish our own lock from a peer's at re-read/release time, so on a
327:       // stamp failure fall through to the contention path and retry rather than
328:       // claim an unverifiable lock.
329:       let stamped = false;
330:       try {
331:         fs.writeSync(fd, JSON.stringify({ pid: process.pid, ts: Date.now(), nonce }));
332:         stamped = true;
333:       } catch {
334:         // Could not stamp; treat as not-acquired and recover below.
335:       }
336:       if (!stamped) {
337:         // Release the half-formed lock so a peer (or our own retry) can take it,
338:         // then loop to re-acquire cleanly.
339:         try {
340:           fs.closeSync(fd);
341:         } catch {
342:           // best-effort
343:         }
344:         fd = undefined;
345:         try {
346:           // Only remove if it is the file we just (unstamped) created. Empty
347:           // body → readLockNonce returns null → not ours, so skip; otherwise a
348:           // peer beat us between open and here.
349:           if (readLockNonce(lockPath) === null) fs.rmSync(lockPath, { force: true });
350:         } catch {
351:           // best-effort
352:         }
353:         const remainingMs = deadline - Date.now();
354:         if (maxWaitMs <= 0 || remainingMs <= 0) {
355:           const lockErr = new Error('deep-loop graph writer lock could not be stamped');
356:           lockErr.code = 'DB_ERROR';
357:           throw lockErr;
358:         }
359:         sleepSync(Math.min(retryIntervalMs, remainingMs));
360:         continue;
361:       }
362:       // P0 close: re-read the file we just stamped. If a peer reclaimed (rmSync)
363:       // our lock and recreated its own in the window between our openSync and
364:       // this read, the on-disk nonce will not be ours — we are NOT the holder.
365:       // Close our (now unlinked or foreign) fd and loop to re-evaluate, rather
366:       // than entering the critical section on a lock someone else owns.
367:       if (readLockNonce(lockPath) !== nonce) {
368:         try {
369:           fs.closeSync(fd);
370:         } catch {
371:           // best-effort
372:         }
373:         fd = undefined;
374:         const remainingMs = deadline - Date.now();
375:         if (maxWaitMs <= 0 || remainingMs <= 0) {
376:           const lockErr = new Error('deep-loop graph writer lock is held by another process');
377:           lockErr.code = 'DB_ERROR';
378:           throw lockErr;
379:         }
380:         sleepSync(Math.min(retryIntervalMs, remainingMs));
381:         continue;
382:       }
383:       break;
384:     } catch (err) {
385:       const code = err && typeof err === 'object' && 'code' in err ? err.code : undefined;
386:       if (code !== 'EEXIST') throw err;
387: 
388:       // Stale-lock reclamation: if the holder is dead or the lock has aged out,
389:       // remove it and retry the open. This can itself race a second reclaimer,
390:       // so loop — on a re-thrown EEXIST we re-evaluate staleness next pass.
391:       // Bounded by a reclaim-attempt cap so a pathological never-removable lock
392:       // (or a peer that keeps re-stamping a stale ts) cannot spin forever.
393:       if (reclaimAttempts < MAX_RECLAIM_ATTEMPTS && isWriterLockStale(lockPath, maxHoldMs)) {
394:         reclaimAttempts += 1;
395:         try {
396:           fs.rmSync(lockPath, { force: true });
397:         } catch {
398:           // Another reclaimer may have removed it first; the retry settles it.
399:         }
400:         continue;
401:       }
402: 
403:       const remainingMs = deadline - Date.now();
404:       if (maxWaitMs <= 0 || remainingMs <= 0) {
405:         const lockErr = new Error('deep-loop graph writer lock is held by another process');
406:         lockErr.code = 'DB_ERROR';
407:         throw lockErr;
408:       }
409:       sleepSync(Math.min(retryIntervalMs, remainingMs));
410:     }
411:   }
412: 
413:   return () => {
414:     if (typeof fd === 'number') {
415:       try {
416:         fs.closeSync(fd);
417:       } catch {
418:         // Best-effort cleanup during process teardown.
419:       }
420:       fd = undefined;
421:     }
422:     // P1 close: only remove the lock if its on-disk body still carries OUR
423:     // nonce. If we were reclaimed while holding the critical section (our write
424:     // aged past maxHoldMs, or the P0 race), the file now belongs to a new
425:     // holder — deleting it would break their mutual exclusion. A missing,
426:     // half-written, or foreign-nonce file all read as "not mine" → skip rmSync.
427:     try {
428:       if (readLockNonce(lockPath) === nonce) {
429:         fs.rmSync(lockPath, { force: true });
430:       }
431:     } catch {
432:       // Best-effort: never throw from release/teardown.
433:     }
434:   };
435: }
436: 
437: // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
438: // 5. EXPORTS
439: // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
440: 
441: module.exports = {
442:   acquireWriterLock,
443:   classifyExitCode,
444:   installSignalHandlers,
445:   maybeThrowTestFault,
446:   sleepSync,
447:   validateNamespaceValue,
448: };

221: ### @deep-research — Technical Investigator
222: 
223: | Property           | Value                                                           |
224: | ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
225: | **Role**           | Iterative evidence gathering, feasibility analysis, technology comparison |
226: | **Model**          | gpt-5.5 (reasoning effort: `high` recommended for synthesis)    |
227: | **Sandbox Mode**   | workspace-write                                                 |
228: | **Modifies Files** | Yes (research.md)                                               |
229: 
230: **Best for:** Technology comparison, API research, architecture exploration, external documentation lookup via web browsing.
231: 
232: **Delegate when:** You need live web information for framework documentation, external API details, or current best practices.
233: 
234: **Unique capability:** `--search` flag enables real-time web browsing.
235: 
236: ```bash
237: codex exec -p research --search -s workspace-write \
238:   "Research the latest Next.js 15 App Router migration patterns. Compare with current Remix approach in this codebase." \
239:   --model gpt-5.5
240: ```
241: 
242: ---
243: 
244: ### @review — Code Quality Guardian
245: 
246: | Property           | Value                                                 |
247: | ------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------- |
248: | **Role**           | Code review, quality scoring (0-100), security audits |
249: | **Model**          | gpt-5.5 (reasoning effort: `high` for deep review/security, `medium` for standard review) |
250: | **Sandbox Mode**   | read-only                                             |
251: | **Modifies Files** | Never                                                 |
252: 
253: **Best for:** Cross-AI code review (second opinion), security audits, quality scoring with P0/P1/P2 severity classification.
254: 
255: **Delegate when:** You want a second perspective on code quality, or need to validate the calling AI's own implementation from a different model's viewpoint.
256: 
257: ```bash
258: codex exec -p review \
259:   "@src/auth/handler.ts @src/auth/middleware.ts Review these files for security vulnerabilities and code quality" \
260:   -s read-only --model gpt-5.5 > /tmp/review-output.txt
261: ```
262: 
263: ---
264: 
265: ### @ai-council — Multi-Strategy Planner
266: 
267: | Property           | Value                                                |
268: | ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- |
269: | **Role**           | Multi-strategy planning with diverse thinking lenses |
270: | **Model**          | gpt-5.5 (reasoning effort: `high` / `xhigh` for complex planning) |
271: | **Sandbox Mode**   | read-only                                            |
272: | **Modifies Files** | Never                                                |
273: 
274: **Best for:** Architecture decisions, complex planning requiring multiple perspectives (Analytical, Creative, Critical, Pragmatic, Holistic lenses).
275: 
276: **Delegate when:** You need a fundamentally different planning approach from a different model, or want to compare Codex's architectural thinking with the calling AI's plan.
277: 
278: ```bash
279: codex exec -p ai-council -s read-only \
280:   "Design the caching strategy for this API. Consider Redis, in-memory, and CDN approaches." \
281:   --model gpt-5.5 > /tmp/ai-council-output.txt
282: ```
283: 
284: ---
285: 
286: 
287: ### Capturing exec Output
288: 
289: ```bash
290: # Capture to file
291: codex exec -p review "@src/auth.ts Review for issues" \
292:   --model gpt-5.5 > /tmp/review.txt
293: 
294: # Capture to variable
295: RESULT=$(codex exec -p context "List all exported functions" \
296:   --model gpt-5.5)
297: 
298: # Check result
299: if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
300:   echo "Success: $RESULT"
301: else
302:   echo "Codex exec failed" >&2
303: fi
304: ```
305: 
306: ### Requesting Structured Output
307: 
308: ```bash
309: # Ask for JSON in the prompt (no native --output flag)
310: codex exec -p review \
311:   "@src/auth.ts Review and return JSON: {score: number, issues: [{line, severity, description}]}" \
312:   --model gpt-5.5 > /tmp/review.json
313: 
314: # Parse with jq
315: jq '.issues[] | select(.severity == "critical")' /tmp/review.json
316: ```
317: 
318: ### Error Handling
319: 
320: | Scenario            | Detection                           | Recovery                                              |
321: | ------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
322: | Auth failure        | Non-zero exit, auth error in output | Re-run `codex login` or check `OPENAI_API_KEY`        |
323: | Profile not found   | Error: profile not defined          | Verify profile name matches `[profiles.<name>]` in config.toml |
324: | Sandbox restriction | Permission denied in output         | Upgrade sandbox mode or adjust task scope             |
325: | Timeout / hung      | No output within expected time      | Simplify task scope; break into smaller steps         |
326: | Low-quality output  | Calling AI validation fails        | Retry with refined prompt; use different agent        |
327: 

(End of file - total 327 lines)
</content>

metadata.preview:
### @deep-research — Technical Investigator

| Property           | Value                                                           |
| ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Role**           | Iterative evidence gathering, feasibility analysis, technology comparison |
| **Model**          | gpt-5.5 (reasoning effort: `high` recommended for synthesis)    |
| **Sandbox Mode**   | workspace-write                                                 |
| **Modifies Files** | Yes (research.md)                                               |

**Best for:** Technology comparison, API research, architecture exploration, external documentation lookup via web browsing.

**Delegate when:** You need live web information for framework documentation, external API details, or current best practices.

**Unique capability:** `--search` flag enables real-time web browsing.

```bash
codex exec -p research --search -s workspace-write \
  "Research the latest Next.js 15 App Router migration patterns. Compare with current Remix approach in this codebase." \
  --model gpt-5.5
```

display.text:
### @deep-research — Technical Investigator

| Property           | Value                                                           |
| ------------------ | --------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Role**           | Iterative evidence gathering, feasibility analysis, technology comparison |
| **Model**          | gpt-5.5 (reasoning effort: `high` recommended for synthesis)    |
| **Sandbox Mode**   | workspace-write                                                 |
| **Modifies Files** | Yes (research.md)                                               |

**Best for:** Technology comparison, API research, architecture exploration, external documentation lookup via web browsing.

**Delegate when:** You need live web information for framework documentation, external API details, or current best practices.

**Unique capability:** `--search` flag enables real-time web browsing.

```bash
codex exec -p research --search -s workspace-write \
  "Research the latest Next.js 15 App Router migration patterns. Compare with current Remix approach in this codebase." \
  --model gpt-5.5
```

---

### @review — Code Quality Guardian

| Property           | Value                                                 |
| ------------------ | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| **Role**           | Code review, quality scoring (0-100), security audits |
| **Model**          | gpt-5.5 (reasoning effort: `high` for deep review/security, `medium` for standard review) |
| **Sandbox Mode**   | read-only                                             |
| **Modifies Files** | Never                                                 |

**Best for:** Cross-AI code review (second opinion), security audits, quality scoring with P0/P1/P2 severity classification.

**Delegate when:** You want a second perspective on code quality, or need to validate the calling AI's own implementation from a different model's viewpoint.

```bash
codex exec -p review \
  "@src/auth/handler.ts @src/auth/middleware.ts Review these files for security vulnerabilities and code quality" \
  -s read-only --model gpt-5.5 > /tmp/review-output.txt
```

---

### @ai-council — Multi-Strategy Planner

| Property           | Value                                                |
| ------------------ | ---------------------------------------------------- |
| **Role**           | Multi-strategy planning with diverse thinking lenses |
| **Model**          | gpt-5.5 (reasoning effort: `high` / `xhigh` for complex planning) |
| **Sandbox Mode**   | read-only                                            |
| **Modifies Files** | Never                                                |

**Best for:** Architecture decisions, complex planning requiring multiple perspectives (Analytical, Creative, Critical, Pragmatic, Holistic lenses).

**Delegate when:** You need a fundamentally different planning approach from a different model, or want to compare Codex's architectural thinking with the calling AI's plan.

```bash
codex exec -p ai-council -s read-only \
  "Design the caching strategy for this API. Consider Redis, in-memory, and CDN approaches." \
  --model gpt-5.5 > /tmp/ai-council-output.txt
```

---


### Capturing exec Output

```bash
# Capture to file
codex exec -p review "@src/auth.ts Review for issues" \
  --model gpt-5.5 > /tmp/review.txt

# Capture to variable
RESULT=$(codex exec -p context "List all exported functions" \
  --model gpt-5.5)

# Check result
if [ $? -eq 0 ]; then
  echo "Success: $RESULT"
else
  echo "Codex exec failed" >&2
fi
```

### Requesting Structured Output

```bash
# Ask for JSON in the prompt (no native --output flag)
codex exec -p review \
  "@src/auth.ts Review and return JSON: {score: number, issues: [{line, severity, description}]}" \
  --model gpt-5.5 > /tmp/review.json

# Parse with jq
jq '.issues[] | select(.severity == "critical")' /tmp/review.json
```

### Error Handling

| Scenario            | Detection                           | Recovery                                              |
| ------------------- | ----------------------------------- | ----------------------------------------------------- |
| Auth failure        | Non-zero exit, auth error in output | Re-run `codex login` or check `OPENAI_API_KEY`        |
| Profile not found   | Error: profile not defined          | Verify profile name matches `[profiles.<name>]` in config.toml |
| Sandbox restriction | Permission denied in output         | Upgrade sandbox mode or adjust task scope             |
| Timeout / hung      | No output within expected time      | Simplify task scope; break into smaller steps         |
| Low-quality output  | Calling AI validation fails        | Retry with refined prompt; use different agent        |

 ### The Two Non-Obvious Rules
 
 Two `opencode run` defaults punish operators who learn them the hard way.
 
-**Rule 1: never pass a top-level `--agent`.** Current OpenCode treats named agents like `general` as subagents and rejects them at the top level, so `--agent general` fails the dispatch outright. When no `--agent` is given, the default agent runs, which is what you want for nearly every dispatch. State any agent-profile request in the prompt body, for example "Act as a code-review agent: ...".
+**Rule 1: use `--agent` deliberately.** `opencode run` supports `--agent <slug>` and the cli-opencode reference uses per-use-case agents such as `context`, `orchestrate`, `review`, `debug` and `ai-council`. Do not pass `--agent general`: the default/general path is selected by omitting `--agent`, and some small-model paths documented in `SKILL.md` also carry narrower omit-agent caveats.
 
 **Rule 2: append `</dev/null` to every non-interactive run.** Without closed stdin, `opencode run` hangs at zero percent CPU after the snapshot-cleanup line. A foreground `| tail` happens to bypass this because the upstream pipe stage provides closed stdin, but `> stdout.log 2> stderr.log` does not. Position `</dev/null` after the prompt argument, before the redirects.
 
 ### The Self-Invocation Guard
@@ -158,9 +159,9 @@
 | What you see | Why | Fix |
 |---|---|---|
 | `command not found: opencode` | CLI not installed or PATH not updated | `brew install opencode` or `curl -fsSL https://opencode.ai/install \| bash`, then restart your terminal |
 | Dispatch hangs at 0% CPU after the snapshot line | Missing `</dev/null` on a non-interactive run | Append `</dev/null` before any `> stdout.log 2> stderr.log` redirect |
-| `--agent general` fails or warns | Current OpenCode rejects named agents at the top level | Omit `--agent`. State the agent profile in the prompt body. |
+| `--agent general` fails or warns | The default/general path is selected by omitting `--agent` | Omit `--agent general`; use concrete slugs like `context`, `orchestrate`, `review`, `debug` or `ai-council` when routing to a specialist. |
 | `provider/model not found` or `401 Unauthorized` | The default `opencode-go` is not configured on this machine | Run `opencode providers list`, then `opencode providers login <provider>` for the missing one |
 | Empty event stream | Output format defaulted to formatted instead of JSON | Force `--format json` |
 | `Self-invocation refused` | The caller is already inside OpenCode (`OPENCODE_*` env, `opencode` ancestry or a state lock) | Use a different runtime, exit the current session or restate with explicit parallel-detached keywords |
 | Unknown `--share` or `--variant` flag | Binary older than the documented baseline | Run `opencode --version` and `opencode run --help`, then upgrade |

 A: One: `deep-loop-workflows`, across its five modes (`context`, `research`, `review`, `ai-council` and `improvement`). Each mode imports the modules it needs and calls the `.cjs` scripts from its workflow YAML. The runtime itself has no user-facing command and registers no MCP tools.

-A: A session-scoped SQLite graph that tracks research nodes (questions, findings, claims, sources) and review nodes (dimensions, files, findings, evidence, bugs, invariants). +A: A session-scoped SQLite graph that tracks research nodes (questions, findings, claims, sources) and review nodes (dimensions, files, findings, evidence, bugs, invariants). Query builders surface uncovered questions, unverified claims and contradictions. Signal extractors feed `convergence.cjs` with typed research, review and context metrics such as `questionCoverage`, `claimVerificationRate`, `contradictionDensity`, `sliceCoverage`, `reuseCatalogCoverage` and `agreementRate`.

-A: It reads per-iteration novelty signals from the coverage graph and returns a typed decision: CONTINUE, STOP_ALLOWED or STOP_BLOCKED. It uses Laplace smoothing and a configurable weight matrix, and each consumer sets its own convergence thresholds on top of the shared scorer.

 **Q: Why are council primitives here rather than in the `ai-council` mode?**
 
 A: Multi-seat dispatch, round-state JSONL, adjudicator scoring, cost guards and session-state hierarchy are durability contracts, not UX. They belong with the other deep-loop durability primitives. The `ai-council` mode owns the operator-facing council semantics, and the split keeps it free to change its UX without touching the durability layer.

 ## 1. OVERVIEW
 
-Persistence layer for deep-loop graph state. Read and written by `scripts/*.cjs` entry points via the `lib/coverage-graph/` schema.

 ## 2. FILES
 
 | File | Purpose |
 |------|---------|
 | `deep-loop-graph.sqlite` | SQLite database holding coverage-graph state across deep-* sessions |

+- Graph writer locks are implemented by `scripts/lib/cli-guards.cjs` `acquireWriterLock` using `wx` lock-file acquisition, stale-lock reclamation and nonce ownership checks

 - Schema: `lib/coverage-graph/`
-- Atomic-state contract: `lib/deep-loop/`
+- Council schema: `lib/council/council-graph-db.ts`
+- Writer-lock contract: `scripts/lib/cli-guards.cjs`

 **Step 4: Spawn a parallel detached session (inside OpenCode only).**
 
 ```bash
@@ -99,9 +100,9 @@
 ### The Two Non-Obvious Rules
 
 Two `opencode run` defaults punish operators who learn them the hard way.
 
-**Rule 1: never pass a top-level `--agent`.** Current OpenCode treats named agents like `general` as subagents and rejects them at the top level, so `--agent general` fails the dispatch outright. When no `--agent` is given, the default agent runs, which is what you want for nearly every dispatch. State any agent-profile request in the prompt body, for example "Act as a code-review agent: ...".
+**Rule 1: use `--agent` deliberately.** `opencode run` supports `--agent <slug>` and the cli-opencode reference uses per-use-case agents such as `context`, `orchestrate`, `review`, `debug` and `ai-council`. Do not pass `--agent general`: the default/general path is selected by omitting `--agent`, and some small-model paths documented in `SKILL.md` also carry narrower omit-agent caveats.
 
 **Rule 2: append `</dev/null` to every non-interactive run.** Without closed stdin, `opencode run` hangs at zero percent CPU after the snapshot-cleanup line. A foreground `| tail` happens to bypass this because the upstream pipe stage provides closed stdin, but `> stdout.log 2> stderr.log` does not. Position `</dev/null` after the prompt argument, before the redirects.
 
 ### The Self-Invocation Guard
@@ -158,9 +159,9 @@
 | What you see | Why | Fix |
 |---|---|---|
 | `command not found: opencode` | CLI not installed or PATH not updated | `brew install opencode` or `curl -fsSL https://opencode.ai/install \| bash`, then restart your terminal |
 | Dispatch hangs at 0% CPU after the snapshot line | Missing `</dev/null` on a non-interactive run | Append `</dev/null` before any `> stdout.log 2> stderr.log` redirect |
-| `--agent general` fails or warns | Current OpenCode rejects named agents at the top level | Omit `--agent`. State the agent profile in the prompt body. |
+| `--agent general` fails or warns | The default/general path is selected by omitting `--agent` | Omit `--agent general`; use concrete slugs like `context`, `orchestrate`, `review`, `debug` or `ai-council` when routing to a specialist. |
 | `provider/model not found` or `401 Unauthorized` | The default `opencode-go` is not configured on this machine | Run `opencode providers list`, then `opencode providers login <provider>` for the missing one |
 | Empty event stream | Output format defaulted to formatted instead of JSON | Force `--format json` |
 | `Self-invocation refused` | The caller is already inside OpenCode (`OPENCODE_*` env, `opencode` ancestry or a state lock) | Use a different runtime, exit the current session or restate with explicit parallel-detached keywords |
 | Unknown `--share` or `--variant` flag | Binary older than the documented baseline | Run `opencode --version` and `opencode run --help`, then upgrade |

display.text:
When any layer matches, the skill returns a refusal and loads nothing. The cli-X family exists for cross-AI delegation. A running CLI skill never dispatches itself.

### Agent Routing And Reasoning Effort

Route to a specialized Codex agent through the repo config. `.codex/config.toml` declares `[agents.<name>]` entries whose `config_file` values point at `.codex/agents/<name>.toml`; the repo-local Codex-side roster is larger than the old six-profile set.

| Agent | Task | Pair with |
|---|---|---|
| `code` | Application-code implementation | `--sandbox workspace-write` |
| `context` | Production context retrieval and codebase mapping | `--sandbox read-only` |
| `debug` | Fresh-perspective debugging | `--sandbox workspace-write` |
| `deep-context` | Deep context analysis | `--sandbox read-only` |
| `deep-improvement` | Proposal-only improvement candidates | `--sandbox read-only` |
| `deep-research` | Iterative technical research | `--search` |
| `deep-review` | Iterative code review | `--sandbox read-only` |
| `markdown` | Documentation execution | `--sandbox workspace-write` |
| `orchestrate` | Multi-agent coordination | `--sandbox read-only` |
| `prompt-improver` | Dispatch-ready prompt packages | `--sandbox read-only` |
| `review` | Code review and security audit | `--sandbox read-only` |
| `ai-council` | Multi-strategy planning | `--sandbox read-only` |

The model stays `gpt-5.5` for every task. Only reasoning effort changes, set with `-c model_reasoning_effort="<level>"` across `none`, `minimal`, `low`, `medium` (default), `high` and `xhigh`. There is no `--reasoning-effort` flag.

### Auth Pre-Flight And Memory Handback

Before the first dispatch the skill checks whether `OPENAI_API_KEY` is set or ChatGPT OAuth is configured. If the key is missing but OAuth exists, it asks before substituting. If neither is configured, it surfaces the login commands and waits. The two options are `export OPENAI_API_KEY=sk-...` and `codex login`. When the caller needs to keep a Codex session's context, a 7-step Memory Handback extracts it and persists it through `generate-context.js` (full procedure in `system-spec-kit/references/cli/memory_handback.md`).

---

## 5. INTEGRATION & NAVIGATION

### When To Use This Skill

Reach for cli-codex when a task benefits from sandboxed file operations, live web search via `--search`, the diff-aware review subcommand or a second-AI opinion on code quality. Reach for it too when you want to offload generation to a sandboxed runtime while you keep conducting. Skip it for simple tasks the caller can answer directly, for interactive terminal work (use `codex` directly) and for deep extended-thinking analysis (use `cli-claude-code` instead).

### Sibling Boundaries
